data.title: Forgotten password
data.solution:
numbers = list(map(int, input().split()))
passwords = set()

for i in range(3):
    for j in range(3):
        password = numbers[0]
        match i:
            case 0:
                password += numbers[1]
            case 1:
                password -= numbers[1]
            case 2:
                password *= numbers[1]

        match j:
            case 0:
                password += numbers[2]
            case 1:
                password -= numbers[2]
            case 2:
                password *= numbers[2]
        
        if password >= 0 and len(str(password)) in [3, 4]:
            passwords.add(password)

for password in sorted(passwords):
    print(password)
data.statement:
Baptist has forgotten his password. Fortunately, he wrote {{three}} numbers [[num]] on a piece of paper, remembering that his password is a combination of these numbers and the operators +, - and *.

He did an operation using the first two numbers, and then another one between the result of the first operation and the remaining number.

The two operations may be the same or different.

For example, if the numbers are 89, 64 and 10, some of the possibilities are:
• (89 + 64) + 10 = 163
• (89 + 64) * 10 = 1530
• (89 - 64) * 10 = 250
• (89 * 64) - 10 = 5686

He wants to try all possible passwords to find the correct one.
His password can be either a <<3- or 4-digits>> number; and it <<cannot be a negative number>>.
Can you help him by printing all the possible ones in numerical order?
data.constraints:
- It is guaranteed that there is always at least one possible password.
- 1 ≤ each [[num]] ≤ 1000
data.inputDescription: <<One line>> : The {{3}} numbers [[num]] Baptist has written, separated by spaces.
data.outputDescription: One line per possible password, in numerical order.

lastVersion.statementHTML:
Goal

Baptist has forgotten his password. Fortunately, he wrote three numbers num on a piece of paper, remembering that his password is a combination of these numbers and the operators +, - and *.

He did an operation using the first two numbers, and then another one between the result of the first operation and the remaining number.

The two operations may be the same or different.

For example, if the numbers are 89, 64 and 10, some of the possibilities are:
• (89 + 64) + 10 = 163
• (89 + 64) * 10 = 1530
• (89 - 64) * 10 = 250
• (89 * 64) - 10 = 5686

He wants to try all possible passwords to find the correct one.
His password can be either a 3- or 4-digits number; and it cannot be a negative number.
Can you help him by printing all the possible ones in numerical order?
Input
One line : The 3 numbers num Baptist has written, separated by spaces.
Output
One line per possible password, in numerical order.
Constraints
- It is guaranteed that there is always at least one possible password.
- 1 ≤ each num ≤ 1000
Example
Input
10 2 8
Output
160
